column7: Formalises the Byzantine Generals Problem; proves deterministic BGP is solvable iff n >= 3f+1 and requires at least f+1 rounds.
column9: Fault threshold (n, f), round complexity (f+1 lower bound).
column10: Abstract; no real network model; no performance measurement.
column11: Establishes the 3f+1 bound that every BFT family in this thesis inherits.
column12: L. Lamport, R. Shostak, and M. Pease, “The Byzantine Generals Problem,” ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, vol. 4, no. 3, pp. 382–401, 1982.

column7: FLP impossibility: no deterministic protocol can guarantee consensus under asynchrony even with a single crash fault.
column10: No constructive protocol; motivates rather than provides a solution.
column11: Justifies why every family studied here relaxes asynchrony, uses randomness, or adds economic incentives.
column12: M. J. Fischer, N. A. Lynch, and M. S. Paterson, “Impossibility of Distributed Consensus with One Faulty Process,” Journal of the ACM, vol. 32, no. 2, pp. 374–382, 1985.

column7: Introduces the partial-synchrony model; shows consensus is solvable under partial synchrony with f < n/3.
column9: Bound on f under partial synchrony.
column10: Does not measure real latency/throughput; model rather than system.
column11: Defines the assumption that PBFT, HotStuff, Tendermint, Casper FFG all adopt.
column12: C. Dwork, N. Lynch, and L. Stockmeyer, “Consensus in the Presence of Partial Synchrony,” Journal of the ACM, vol. 35, no. 2, pp. 288–323, 1988.

column7: PBFT: first practical BFT state-machine replication protocol with three-phase commit and view change.
column9: End-to-end latency (ms on LAN), throughput (ops/s), message complexity O(n^2).
column10: LAN scale (<= 4 replicas evaluated); no WAN / adversarial-delay evaluation.
column11: Canonical baseline for the PBFT family implemented in the simulator.
column12: M. Castro and B. Liskov, “Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance,” in Proc. 3rd USENIX Symp. Operating Systems Design and Implementation (OSDI), 1999, pp. 173–186.

column7: HotStuff: linearises view change to O(n) via threshold signatures; enables responsive leader rotation.
column9: Message complexity (O(n)), latency (rounds), throughput under increasing n.
column10: Measures synchronous environments; limited adversarial delay modelling.
column11: Provides the pipelined/linearised PBFT variant used as an optimisation baseline.
column12: M. Yin, D. Malkhi, M. K. Reiter, G. G. Gueta, and I. Abraham, “HotStuff: BFT Consensus with Linearity and Responsiveness,” in Proc. ACM Symp. Principles of Distributed Computing (PODC), 2019, pp. 347–356.

column7: Tendermint: round-robin leader BFT with locking rule; basis of Cosmos SDK chains.
column10: Not peer-reviewed journal venue; production data anecdotal.
column11: Reference for simplified PBFT-family implementation with round-robin leaders in the simulator.
column12: E. Buchman, J. Kwon, and Z. Milosevic, “The Latest Gossip on BFT Consensus,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1807.04938, 2018.

column7: Casper FFG: BFT finality gadget overlay on a blockchain; accountable safety via slashing.
column10: Does not measure implementation performance; accountability proof only.
column11: Canonical PoS-finality reference for the simulator's finality-gadget module.
column12: V. Buterin and V. Griffith, “Casper the Friendly Finality Gadget,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1710.09437, 2017.

column3: Buterin, Hernandez, Kamphefner, Pham, Qiao, Ryan, Sin, Wang, Zhang
column7: Gasper: combines LMD-GHOST fork choice with Casper FFG finality; full Ethereum 2 PoS protocol.
column9: Time-to-finality (epochs), reorg depth under attestation delay.
column10: No standalone empirical evaluation; analyses are partial.
column11: Reference for fork-choice interaction with finality in PoS simulator experiments.
column12: V. Buterin et al., “Combining GHOST and Casper,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2003.03052, 2020.

column3: Team Rocket, Yin, Sekniqi, van Renesse, Sirer
column5: arXiv (widely cited; basis of Avalanche network)
column7: Snowflake-to-Avalanche family: probabilistic BFT via repeated random subsampling; tunable finality epsilon.
column9: Time-to-finality (~1.35 s reported), throughput (~3.4 ktps reported on testnet), safety probability vs Byzantine fraction.
column10: Original analysis informal; later formal work has refined and partially weakened the bounds.
column11: Canonical reference for the Avalanche-style sampling protocol implemented in the simulator.
column12: Team Rocket, M. Yin, K. Sekniqi, R. van Renesse, and E. G. Sirer, “Scalable and Probabilistic Leaderless BFT Consensus through Metastability,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1906.08936, 2019.

column7: Rigorous analysis of Avalanche that identifies conditions under which liveness degrades more than previously claimed.
column9: Safety probability bounds; liveness conditions vs adversary strength.
column11: Critical companion to [9] for balanced treatment of Avalanche's security claims in Chapter 2.
column12: I. Amores-Sesar, C. Cachin, and P. Schneider, “An Analysis of Avalanche Consensus,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2401.02811, 2024.

column7: Narwhal + Tusk: DAG-based mempool separates data availability from ordering; throughput ~140 ktps reported.
column9: Throughput (ktps), end-to-end latency (seconds), message count per block.
column10: Throughput numbers depend on batching and hardware; workload-specific.
column11: Reference implementation target for the DAG-based module in the simulator.
column12: G. Danezis, L. Kokoris-Kogias, A. Sonnino, and A. Spiegelman, “Narwhal and Tusk: A DAG-based Mempool and Efficient BFT Consensus,” in Proc. 17th European Conference on Computer Systems (EuroSys), 2022, pp. 34–50.

column7: Bullshark: partially-synchronous fast-path DAG BFT with 2-round commit; simpler than Narwhal/Tusk.
column8: Protocol design + implementation (~200 LoC on Narwhal)
column9: Commit latency (rounds), throughput under synchrony, simplicity LoC.
column10: Not peer-reviewed formal safety proof in main paper; asynchronous fallback complex.
column11: Refines the DAG-based implementation strategy for the simulator's DAG module.
column12: A. Spiegelman, N. Giridharan, A. Sonnino, and L. Kokoris-Kogias, “Bullshark: DAG BFT Protocols Made Practical,” in Proc. ACM Conf. Computer and Communications Security (CCS), 2022, pp. 2705–2718.

column7: Mysticeti: uncertified DAG consensus reaching the 3-round BFT latency lower bound; >200 ktps, ~0.5 s WAN latency reported.
column8: Protocol design + WAN prototype + production deployment on Sui
column10: Production-centric; vendor-reported numbers; newer protocol with limited independent verification.
column11: Current state-of-the-art DAG-based reference for upper-bound throughput comparison in Chapter 5.
column12: K. Babel, A. Chursin, G. Danezis, L. Kokoris-Kogias, and A. Sonnino, “Mysticeti: Reaching the Latency Limits with Uncertified DAGs,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2310.14821, 2023.

column3: Bano, Sonnino, Al-Bassam, Azouvi, McCorry, Meiklejohn, Danezis
column7: SoK: systematises blockchain consensus into PoW, proof-of-X, and hybrid/classical BFT families; defines evaluation axes.
column9: Taxonomy of security and performance axes (throughput, latency, fault model, incentive).
column10: Published in 2019; excludes recent DAG-based and Avalanche developments.
column11: Primary taxonomic anchor for Chapter 2's literature framing.
column12: S. Bano, A. Sonnino, M. Al-Bassam, S. Azouvi, P. McCorry, S. Meiklejohn, and G. Danezis, “SoK: Consensus in the Age of Blockchains,” in Proc. 1st ACM Conf. Advances in Financial Technologies (AFT), 2019, pp. 183–198.

column7: Comprehensive survey comparing Nakamoto, PoS, BFT-based and hybrid consensus protocols with quantitative metrics.
column9: Throughput (tps), latency, fault tolerance, scalability ranges per family.
column10: Pre-dates Narwhal/Tusk/Mysticeti; quantitative ranges are aggregated across inconsistent source measurements.
column11: Primary source of reported-metric ranges for the comparative table in the thesis.
column12: Y. Xiao, N. Zhang, W. Lou, and Y. T. Hou, “A Survey of Distributed Consensus Protocols for Blockchain Networks,” IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials, vol. 22, no. 2, pp. 1432–1465, 2020.

column7: Reviews consensus protocols in permissioned blockchains; advocates formal models and public review of BFT claims.
column9: Qualitative comparison of fault model, trust model, safety/liveness.
column11: Methodological anchor: motivates the rigorous evaluation that this thesis performs quantitatively.
column12: C. Cachin and M. Vukolic, “Blockchain Consensus Protocols in the Wild,” arXiv preprint arXiv:1707.01873, 2017.

column3: Gervais, Karame, Wuest, Glykantzis, Ritzdorf, Capkun
column7: Quantitative framework for PoW security and performance; studies effect of block size and propagation delay on throughput and selfish-mining profitability.
column9: Throughput (tps) vs block interval, stale-block rate, double-spend / selfish-mining success probability.
column10: Focused on PoW; does not cover BFT/PoS/DAG families.
column11: Methodological precedent for simulation-based, metrics-instrumented comparative evaluation — the same approach this thesis applies to BFT families.
column12: A. Gervais, G. O. Karame, K. Wüst, V. Glykantzis, H. Ritzdorf, and S. Capkun, “On the Security and Performance of Proof of Work Blockchains,” in Proc. ACM Conf. Computer and Communications Security (CCS), 2016, pp. 3–16.
